{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient presented with fever, headache, vomiting, disorientation, neck rigidity, and a single episode of generalized tonic-clonic seizures followed by unconsciousness. The CSF examination showed a cell count of 300 cells/mm3 with a predominance of polymorphs (50-70%), protein 70 mg/dL, and glucose 50 mg/dL (blood: 95 mg/dL). These findings are not consistent with pyogenic meningitis, as the glucose level is higher than expected. Tubercular meningitis typically shows a lower glucose level and a predominance of lymphocytes. Herpes encephalitis usually presents with a higher cell count and predominantly lymphocytic pleocytosis. Cerebral malaria is more common in regions where malaria is endemic and presents with different clinical features. Given the CSF findings, the most likely diagnosis is pyogenic meningitis.", "answer_choice": "A"}
